name: ML Types: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning
description: Core machine learning paradigms distinguished by how models learn from data and feedback signals
key_facts: Supervised learning uses labeled data to learn input-output mappings (classification and regression)
Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection)
Reinforcement learning uses reward signals to learn optimal actions in an environment
Semi-supervised learning combines labeled and unlabeled data to improve model accuracy
common_misconceptions: Unsupervised learning does not require any data preparation (it still needs clean, formatted input)
Reinforcement learning always requires a physical environment (it can use simulated environments)

name: Neural Networks Basics
description: Fundamental architecture of artificial neural networks including layers, activation functions, and backpropagation
key_facts: Neural networks consist of input, hidden, and output layers connected by weighted edges
Activation functions (ReLU, sigmoid, softmax) introduce non-linearity into the model
Backpropagation adjusts weights by computing gradients of the loss function
Deep learning refers to neural networks with multiple hidden layers
CNNs excel at image tasks, RNNs at sequential data, and Transformers at language tasks
common_misconceptions: More layers always means better performance (overfitting and vanishing gradients can occur)
Neural networks understand data the way humans do (they learn statistical patterns, not meaning)

name: Training vs Inference
description: Distinction between the model training phase (learning from data) and inference phase (making predictions on new data)
key_facts: Training is compute-intensive and uses GPUs/specialized hardware to learn patterns from data
Inference uses the trained model to generate predictions on new, unseen data
Training is typically a batch process; inference can be real-time or batch
Inference costs often exceed training costs over a model's lifetime
common_misconceptions: Training is always more expensive than inference (inference at scale can cost more over time)
A trained model never needs retraining (models degrade as data distributions shift)

name: Foundation Models
description: Large pre-trained models that can be adapted for a wide variety of downstream tasks through fine-tuning or prompting
key_facts: Foundation models are trained on broad datasets and can be adapted to many tasks
Examples include Claude (Anthropic), Titan (Amazon), Llama (Meta), and Stable Diffusion (Stability AI)
They reduce the need to train models from scratch for each use case
Amazon Bedrock provides access to multiple foundation models via a single API
common_misconceptions: Foundation models are only for text generation (they also handle images, code, embeddings, and more)
All foundation models are the same size and capability (they vary widely in parameters and strengths)

name: Transformers and Large Language Models
description: The transformer architecture powering modern LLMs, using self-attention mechanisms for parallel processing of sequences
key_facts: Transformers use self-attention to weigh the importance of different parts of the input
They process sequences in parallel, unlike RNNs which process sequentially
LLMs are transformer-based models trained on massive text corpora
Tokens are the basic units that LLMs process (words, subwords, or characters)
common_misconceptions: LLMs truly understand language and meaning (they predict statistically likely token sequences)
Bigger models are always better for every task (smaller models can be more efficient and cost-effective)

name: Prompt Engineering
description: Techniques for crafting effective prompts to guide foundation model outputs toward desired results
key_facts: Zero-shot prompting provides no examples; few-shot prompting includes example input-output pairs
Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to show its reasoning step by step
System prompts set the persona, context, and constraints for the model
Temperature controls randomness: lower values for factual tasks, higher for creative tasks
common_misconceptions: Prompt engineering is just about asking a question (structure, context, and examples matter greatly)
The same prompt works equally well across all models (different models respond differently to prompt styles)

name: Fine-Tuning vs RAG
description: Two approaches for customizing foundation model behavior: retraining on domain data vs augmenting with retrieved context
key_facts: Fine-tuning retrains a model on domain-specific data to change its behavior and knowledge
RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and includes them in the prompt context
RAG is preferred when data changes frequently or when source attribution is required
Fine-tuning is preferred for changing the model's style, tone, or specialized task performance
common_misconceptions: RAG and fine-tuning are mutually exclusive (they can be combined for best results)
Fine-tuning permanently teaches a model new facts (it can still hallucinate or forget)

name: Amazon Bedrock
description: Fully managed service for accessing and building with foundation models from multiple providers via a single API
key_facts: Provides access to models from Anthropic, Amazon, Meta, Cohere, Stability AI, and others
Serverless — no infrastructure to manage
Supports text generation, embeddings, image generation, and chat
Includes Knowledge Bases for RAG, Agents for task automation, and Guardrails for safety
Data is not used to train the base models
common_misconceptions: Bedrock only supports Amazon Titan models (it offers models from multiple providers)
You must manage GPU instances to use Bedrock (it is fully managed and serverless)

name: Model Selection
description: Criteria for choosing the right foundation model based on task requirements, cost, latency, and accuracy needs
key_facts: Consider model size, latency requirements, accuracy needs, and cost per token
Smaller models are faster and cheaper but may sacrifice accuracy on complex tasks
Multi-modal models handle text, images, and other inputs together
Amazon Bedrock model evaluation helps compare model performance on your specific tasks
common_misconceptions: The largest model is always the best choice (smaller models often suffice and are more cost-effective)
All models on Bedrock have the same pricing (prices vary significantly across providers and model sizes)

name: Text Generation and Chatbots
description: Using foundation models for content creation, summarization, question answering, and conversational AI applications
key_facts: Common use cases include summarization, translation, code generation, and Q&A
Conversational AI uses context windows to maintain multi-turn dialogue
Amazon Q provides enterprise-grade AI assistants for business and developer use cases
Bedrock Agents can orchestrate multi-step tasks using foundation models and external APIs
common_misconceptions: LLMs always produce factually correct text (hallucination is a known limitation)
Chatbots remember all previous conversations indefinitely (context windows have token limits)

name: Image Generation
description: Using diffusion models and other generative models to create, edit, and transform images from text prompts
key_facts: Diffusion models generate images by iteratively denoising random noise guided by text prompts
Amazon Titan Image Generator creates images and supports inpainting and outpainting
Stability AI models on Bedrock offer Stable Diffusion for image generation
Negative prompts specify what to exclude from generated images
common_misconceptions: Image generation models create images from a database of stored images (they generate from learned patterns)
Generated images are always free to use commercially (licensing depends on the model and training data)

name: Bias Detection and Fairness
description: Identifying and mitigating biases in AI training data and model outputs to ensure fair and equitable outcomes
key_facts: Training data bias occurs when the data does not represent the target population fairly
Amazon SageMaker Clarify detects bias in training data and model predictions
Fairness metrics include demographic parity, equalized odds, and disparate impact
Bias can exist in data collection, feature selection, labeling, and model design
common_misconceptions: Removing protected attributes eliminates bias (proxy variables can still encode bias)
AI models are inherently objective (they reflect biases present in their training data)

name: Explainability and Human Oversight
description: Making AI decisions transparent and interpretable, with human review processes for high-stakes decisions
key_facts: SageMaker Clarify provides feature attribution to explain which inputs influenced predictions
Human-in-the-loop workflows ensure human review for critical AI decisions
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) manages human review workflows for ML predictions
Explainability is required by some regulations such as GDPR for automated decisions
common_misconceptions: All AI models can be fully explained (deep learning models often remain partially opaque)
Human oversight slows down AI to the point of being impractical (it can be applied selectively to edge cases)

name: AI Ethics and Responsible AI Practices
description: Principles and practices for developing and deploying AI systems ethically, including transparency, accountability, and societal impact
key_facts: AWS Responsible AI principles include fairness, explainability, privacy, robustness, and governance
Transparency involves disclosing when users are interacting with AI systems
Accountability requires clear ownership of AI outcomes and defined escalation paths
Impact assessment evaluates potential harms before deploying AI in production
common_misconceptions: Responsible AI only applies to customer-facing applications (internal AI tools also need governance)
Following responsible AI principles guarantees zero harm (it reduces risk but cannot eliminate it)

name: Data Privacy and Model Security for AI
description: Protecting sensitive data in AI systems and securing model access, including encryption, access controls, and compliance
key_facts: Amazon Bedrock encrypts data in transit and at rest; customer data is not used to train base models
IAM policies control who can invoke specific models and access AI resources
VPC endpoints allow private connectivity to Bedrock without internet exposure
AWS CloudTrail logs all Bedrock API calls for auditing and compliance
common_misconceptions: Data sent to Bedrock is used to improve the foundation models (it is not shared with model providers)
AI services do not need the same security controls as other AWS services (standard AWS security applies)

name: Guardrails and Content Filtering
description: Implementing safety boundaries and content moderation for AI applications using Bedrock Guardrails and other controls
key_facts: Bedrock Guardrails filter harmful content, block denied topics, and redact PII
Content filters can be configured for hate speech, violence, sexual content, and profanity
Guardrails can be applied to both input prompts and model responses
Word-level and topic-level filters provide granular control over allowed content
common_misconceptions: Guardrails completely eliminate all harmful outputs (they significantly reduce risk but are not foolproof)
Content filtering is only needed for public-facing applications (internal tools also benefit from safety controls)
